purpose: Decomposes CRE cap rates into four components to identify which drivers have structurally shifted vs cyclically adjusted, enabling more precise forecasting of equilibrium cap rates
components: Risk-free rate (Treasury yield)
Credit spread (borrower default risk premium)
Illiquidity premium (transaction friction and time-to-sale)
Property-specific risk premium (tenant quality lease duration physical obsolescence)
current_assessment: Illiquidity premium and property-specific risk have structurally repriced higher post-COVID. Risk-free component follows rates. Credit spread component widened for office. Equilibrium cap rates of 6.5-7.5% at current long-end rates implying 40-60% of decompression complete
limitations: Components are not independently observable and must be estimated
Property-specific risk for office may be permanently repriced if demand impairment is structural
Historical calibration data may not apply to current structural regime

purpose: Assesses the probability of systemic CRE-banking crisis by decomposing it into the conjunction of 5 independent conditions that must all hold simultaneously
components: Rates must remain elevated
CRE valuations must stay depressed
Lenders must refuse to extend
Alternative capital must be insufficient
Regulatory forbearance must be withdrawn
current_assessment: Joint probability ~3-8% even with generous positive correlation. Each condition has moderate individual probability but conjunction requirement dramatically reduces systemic probability. However a single trigger (recession) could make multiple conditions true simultaneously violating partial-independence
limitations: Partial-independence assumption is the key vulnerability: recession could trigger correlated failure of multiple conditions
Individual condition probabilities are themselves uncertain estimates
Does not capture speed of transition from benign to crisis state

purpose: Sequences CRE distress cycles into five predictable phases to identify current position and forward trajectory
components: Phase 1: Transaction collapse (volume dries up bid-ask spread widens)
Phase 2: Selective price discovery (distressed sales establish new comps)
Phase 3: Credit tightening (lenders restrict new origination)
Phase 4: Forced selling and clearing (maturity wall and forbearance limits force resolution)
Phase 5: Recovery (new capital enters at clearing prices)
current_assessment: Current cycle entered Phase 1 in H2 2022. Currently transitioning through Phase 2-3. Genuine clearing (Phase 4) projected around 2027-2028 based on historical analogues. Won debate vs coarser 4-phase model
limitations: Timing based on n=3-4 observations renders frequentist inference unreliable
Structural novelty of remote work demand shock may invalidate historical timing analogues
Sequencing pattern is robust but specific timing is illustrative not predictive

purpose: Predicts severity of CRE downturns by evaluating the interaction of three key variables
components: Credit expansion magnitude (how much lending preceded the downturn)
Banking system concentration (how concentrated CRE exposure is in vulnerable institutions)
Demand shock type (cyclical vs structural nature of the demand impairment)
current_assessment: Current cycle: moderate credit expansion (less than 2005-07), high banking concentration in regionals, and potentially structural demand shock in office. The structural demand shock component has no clean historical analogue making severity estimation genuinely uncertain
limitations: Small sample of historical episodes for calibration
Demand shock type (structural vs cyclical) is itself the key unresolved question
Interaction effects between variables not well understood

purpose: Quantifies the feasible stock of office-to-residential conversion through successive engineering and economic filters
components: Floor plate depth filter: >65ft eliminates 50-60% of stock
Structural/MEP feasibility filter: eliminates 30-40% of remainder
Zoning and regulatory filter: eliminates 20-30% of what passes physical filters
Economic viability filter: costs of $150-400/sqft require acquisition below $100-200/sqft
current_assessment: Total addressable: $20-50B of ~$3.5T office asset class or less than 1.5%. Conversion is irrelevant to the 2025-2027 maturity wall. Self-referential dynamic: conversion only becomes viable at prices below current marks
limitations: Assumes current building codes and zoning which could change with policy intervention
Cost estimates may vary significantly by market
Does not account for potential technological innovations in conversion methodology

purpose: Identifies the correct instrument panel for monitoring CRE credit stress given that standard credit dashboards miss the CRE debt market entirely
components: CMBX indices (synthetic CMBS credit default swaps)
REIT unsecured bond spreads
Bank quarterly earnings provision language and charge-off trends
FDIC classified asset ratios
CMBS special servicing rates by property type and vintage
Secondary LP interest transaction prices for private CRE funds
Loan sale realization prices
current_assessment: Conventional HY/IG OAS measures are monitoring the wrong population. CMBS special servicing at 7-9% overall but 18-25% for office. CMBX BBB- at 800-1500bp. Anyone using conventional spreads as an all-clear is looking at the wrong instrument panel
limitations: Multiple metrics without clear aggregation methodology
Some metrics have significant reporting lags
Insurance company CRE exposure (~$600-700B) is the largest gap in this measurement architecture

purpose: Maps CRE cycle position to equity factor expected returns to guide portfolio construction during CRE repricing
components: CRE cycle position assessment (using five-phase model)
Value factor CRE exposure calculation (22-25% financials plus 5-7% REITs in Russell 1000 Value)
Regime identification (growth-deceleration and inflation-sticky favors Quality/Low Vol)
Sector drag estimation (15-25% bank drawdown plus REIT repricing creates 4.5-8.5% Value drag)
current_assessment: Current regime strongly favors Quality and Low Volatility over Value. Quality outperforms Value by median 550bp annualized in growth-deceleration inflation-sticky regimes per Kenneth French data 1963-2024
limitations: Single-source framework at this specificity level
Factor timing based on CRE cycle adds implementation noise
Historical factor data may not apply if current CRE cycle is structurally different

purpose: Provides the foundational analytical framework for separating structural demand impairment from cyclical recovery potential in office CRE
components: Cyclical component: interest rate sensitivity normal vacancy oscillation capital markets access
Structural component: remote work adoption secular technology shift in space utilization
Base rate assessment: ~25-35% of structural shift calls in CRE historically prove correct
Monitoring variables: lease rollover outcomes AI impact on collaboration corporate headcount trends
current_assessment: Remote work has stronger evidence than prior structural obsolescence candidates but the base rate for such calls being correct remains low. The answer determines whether office is 20% overbuilt (manageable cyclical) or 50% overbuilt (devastating structural). Requires 3-5 more years of observation data
limitations: Cannot be resolved with current data
Historical base rate for structural shifts may not apply given unprecedented technological enablement of remote work
Decomposition methodology not standardized across analysts
